body_txt: Morality — the categorization of intentions, decisions and actions into "right" and "wrong" — is a huge concept that every human being has to contend with at some point in their existence, from the most basic levels of human survival and self-preservation to the much more widespread scope of interacting with the human biosphere within an extremely diverse universe. "Moral lessons" are often derived from experience in the real world, but narrative tradition and fiction are a valuable vessel to deliver them, as stories can seek to not only entertain, but instruct, inform, or improve their audiences, with characters being used to explore the many functional intricacies, nuanced observations, and differing perspectives explored and documented throughout human experience , whose resulting lessons can be further applied to real life. In this wiki, we call these lessons "Aesops". What exactly constitutes an Aesop depends wildly on the work, as there are seemingly infinite lessons one can derive and impart from the worlds of fiction and non-fiction. How an Aesop is subsequently handled within a story as a storytelling device is itself a complex and multifaceted subject, one which has itself led to many recurring narrative tropes that function regardless of what the actual moral lesson entails. One of the most prominent questions that people focus on regarding Aesops is how clearly the audience is meant to notice them: many stories (especially those intended for younger audiences) explicitly declare their moral lessons , often in a concise proverb or aphorism that's easy to recognize. Others like to be more subtle, usually to encourage the audience to ponder the story's meaning for themselves, often through engaging with its broader themes and metaphors. Aesops derive their name from the ancient Greek storyteller Aesop , who is most known for coining various fables through oral tradition , frequently using Allegorical Characters and simplistic plots to illustrate various lessons. Aesop certainly wasn't the first example of an author using stories to impart (ostensibly) universal wisdom, but he is attributed to one of the most well-known models of storytelling for such a purpose in the modern age , involving simple, proverbial Allegory and containing succinct lessons through adages. Ironically, this modern perception is itself a historical misconception of how Aesop actually told his fables — he would often impart his stories to listeners for them to sort out the meaning for themselves; it's likely that modern compilers tacked on one-liner maxims (such as "slow and steady wins the race"), and later orators often used them as rhetorical devices to make a point. However, highlighting a moral truth was, in fact, one of the main points and characteristics of his fables , and while one was expected to determine them on their own, the stories almost always served to promote a moral message of some sort. Moral lessons being greatly subject to individual interpretation is itself a rich topic of metanarrative discourse. Works are not limited to only offering one moral lesson, let alone the one that was intended by the writer , and that level of disconnect can provoke debates on the nature of the work and how it was created vs. the nature of the audiences, what story qualities are of importance to them, and how they absorb the work. Moral lessons can themselves be fallible and subject to scrutiny within the story's internal logic , they can be discovered without authorial intent , and some lessons can even be derived in a way that's entirely antithetical to what the author was trying to promote due to misinterpretation . Because Aesops are nigh-omnipresent as a shared narrative and interpretive concept, no examples are needed . Examples of individual Aesops/moral lessons within works should be described through corresponding individual tropes, including those within Stock Aesops . Compare with Central Theme , which is a related metanarrative device based around whatever concept, question, or moral idea the plot of the work hinges around. The key distinction is that a Central Theme is what the story is about , while An Aesop is what a story appears to teach to the audience. The Central Theme can and often is used to develop and deliver the Aesop, but they are not, strictly speaking, the same. Aesops Influencing Characters and Plot Devices Aesops Influencing Storytelling Interpretations of Aesops
